title: SEGMENT 11 — The economics of checkpoint inhibitors

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — The economics of checkpoint inhibitors
Let's pause, briefly, on something less biological and more political.
The money.
Pembrolizumab, in 2024, generated approximately twenty-nine and a half billion dollars in global revenue.
Twenty-nine point five billion.
That is more revenue than Coca-Cola or Pepsi makes from any single product line.
It is the single most lucrative pharmaceutical product in human history.
And it shows no sign of slowing — analysts project Keytruda revenue to crest at over thirty billion dollars before patent expiry in 2028.
How does one drug make twenty-nine billion dollars in a year?
Volume and price.
The price, in the United States, is approximately one hundred and seventy-five thousand dollars per patient per year for the standard dosing schedule.

That figure varies by country — the same drug costs about half as much in Western Europe, less in Japan, and much less in low- and middle-income countries through tiered pricing arrangements — but in the U.
S. it is, broadly, that range.
And patients are now treated for one or two years routinely; some indications use it for life.
So we are talking about three hundred and fifty thousand dollars in drug costs for a single course of therapy for a single patient.
The volume is also vast.
As of 2024, more than three million patients worldwide have received pembrolizumab.
It is approved for use across more than forty cancer types and in dozens of distinct settings — first-line, second-line, adjuvant, neoadjuvant, monotherapy, combinations.
The drug has crept into so many corners of oncology that the average comprehensive cancer center now uses it daily.
The economic implications are enormous, and not all of them are good.
The good first.
The revenue has funded a colossal expansion of oncology research.
Merck has plowed Keytruda profits into acquisitions, partnerships, and internal R&D.
Every other major pharmaceutical company, seeing the success, has built or bought immuno-oncology platforms.
The total annual R&D spend on cancer immunotherapy across the industry is now north of fifteen billion dollars.
Hundreds of new combinations and novel targets are in clinical trials.
Whatever you think about pharmaceutical industry margins, the revenue from checkpoint inhibitors has been re-invested into the science, and that re-investment has accelerated the field dramatically.
Now the less good.
Health systems, particularly in the United States, are buckling under the cost.
Medicare's drug spending on cancer immunotherapies is increasing by double-digit percentages annually.
Private insurers are constraining access, requiring prior authorization, narrowing approved indications.
Patients with high copays are facing six-figure out-of-pocket costs that drive them into bankruptcy.

In countries with national health systems, like the UK's NICE, expensive checkpoint inhibitors are approved only for specific indications where cost-effectiveness can be demonstrated — and patients with off-label indications often cannot access them.
The cost is also distributed unequally.
In high-income countries, pembrolizumab is widely available.
In low- and middle-income countries, it remains largely inaccessible, even when the cancers it treats are common.
A patient with MSI-high colorectal cancer in the Bronx and a patient with the same cancer in Lagos do not have the same options.
And then there is the patent cliff.
The composition-of-matter patent for pembrolizumab is currently expected to expire in 2028.
After that — or after various extension strategies run their course — biosimilar versions of the drug will enter the market, and prices will fall, possibly by sixty to eighty percent.
Merck's revenue from Keytruda will collapse.
The whole company will need to absorb a multi-hundred-billion-dollar revenue shortfall over the subsequent decade.
That cliff is driving the most frantic dealmaking in pharmaceutical history.
Merck has made several large acquisitions specifically to acquire next-generation immuno-oncology assets.
They are paying enormous sums for late-stage assets, betting that the next wave — bispecifics, neoantigen vaccines, novel targets — will replace at least some of the lost Keytruda revenue.
Whether they succeed is one of the most-watched commercial questions in biotech.
The science is racing the clock.
The takeaway for our purposes: checkpoint inhibitors are not only a scientific revolution but an economic one.
They have made certain cancers more treatable and certain cancers more expensive than ever.
They are paying for the science that will produce the next generation of therapies.
And they are, all by themselves, reshaping the financial structure of the pharmaceutical industry.
